coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xian-En
Lei, Cuihua
Gao, Yue
Gao, Han
Zhu, Shuyun
Yang, Xue
You, Jinmao
Wang, Hua
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was reported for the first time for the sensitive and selective analysis of Ag+ ions by employing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the reference fluorophore and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the specific recognition probe. Upon the addition of Ag+ ions, OPD could be oxidized to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a strong fluorescence emission at 557nm, whereas the fluorescence of GQDs at 445nm would be simultaneously quenched by the so generated DAP through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). A ratiometric fluorescent Ag+ nanosensor was thus developed. The fluorescence intensity ratios of DAP to GQDs linearly increased with the increasing of Ag+ concentrations in the range of 0–115.2μM, with a detection limit down to 250nM. Furthermore, the feasibility of practical applications of the developed detection strategy for probing Ag+ ions in real water samples was demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor
Silver ions
o-phenylenediamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Ag+ ions
Detection of Ag+ ions in real samples
Ratiometric fluorescent sensing performance for Ag+ ions
Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Ag+ ions
Practical analysis of environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, excitation spectrum of GQDs at λem =445nm, and emission spectrum of GQDs at λex =383nm. (B) Fluorescence spectra of GQDs under the different excitation wavelengths indicated.
(A) The low-magnification TEM image, (B) the size distribution histogram, and (C) the AFM image of GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs (1), GQDs+Ag+ (2), GQDs+OPD (3), and GQDs+OPD+Ag+ (4). [GQDs]=20.0μgmL−1; [OPD]=180μM; [Ag+]=76.8μM. The inset shows the corresponding digital images. (B) The spectral overlap between the fluorescence of GQDs (383nm excitation) and the absorbance spectrum of Ag+-OPD system after incubation for 10min.

Ratiometric Ag+ responses depending on (A) the reaction time between Ag+ ions and OPD, (B) the reaction time between the generated DAP and GQDs, (C) pH values, and (D) the OPD concentrations. [GQDs]=20.0μgmL−1; [Ag+]=96.0μM.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the ratiometric GQDs-OPD responses to Ag+ ions with different concentrations of a-l: 0, 1.2, 2.4, 4.8, 7.2, 9.6, 19.2, 28.8, 38.4, 48.0, 67.2, 76.8, 96.0, and 115.2μM; [GQDs]=20.0μgmL−1; [OPD]=180μM; pH 6.0 of 10mM PBS. (B) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 versus Ag+ concentrations. The inset shows the photographs of GQDs-OPD system in the absence and presence of various amounts of Ag+.
The response selectivity of the ratiometric sensor towards Ag+ ions taking the other metal ions (96.0μM) indicated as the controls. Inset: the corresponding digital photos.
Schematic illustration of the principle and procedure for the determination of Ag+ ions by using GQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for the determination of Ag+ .
Analytical results for Ag+ ions in real water samples.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of silver ions using graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City, Shandong Province, 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Medicine and Materials Applied Technologies, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City, Shandong Province, 273165, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Medicine and Materials Applied Technologies

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Life-Organic Analysis, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qufu Normal University, Qufu City, Shandong Province, 273165, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor has been developed for probing Ag+ ions based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and 2,3-diaminiophenazine (DAP) yielded from the Ag+ ions-oxidized o-phenlyenediamine (OPD).

A ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor was reported for the first time for the sensitive and selective analysis of Ag+ ions by employing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as the reference fluorophore and o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the specific recognition probe. Upon the addition of Ag+ ions, OPD could be oxidized to produce 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with a strong fluorescence emission at 557nm, whereas the fluorescence of GQDs at 445nm would be simultaneously quenched by the so generated DAP through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). A ratiometric fluorescent Ag+ nanosensor was thus developed. The fluorescence intensity ratios of DAP to GQDs linearly increased with the increasing of Ag+ concentrations in the range of 0–115.2μM, with a detection limit down to 250nM. Furthermore, the feasibility of practical applications of the developed detection strategy for probing Ag+ ions in real water samples was demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution caused by heavy metal ions has become an important worldwide concern [1] due to their serious threat to human health even at low concentrations. Silver, a rare but highly demanded and cost-effective metal catalyst, has been widely used in industries, such as electronics, photography, mirrors, and pharmacy [2]. Thousands of tons of Ag+ ions are released to the environmental water media from industrial wastes and emissions annually [3,4]. However, Ag+ ions have been assigned to the highest toxicity class of heavy metal pollution [4]. Therefore, the determination of the concentrations of Ag+ ions in wastewater and aquicolous ecosystem is of great significance [5,6].

Up to now, many analysis methods have been used to detect trace levels of Ag+ ions in aqueous media including atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [7–9], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy [10,11], and ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) [12,13]. However, most of these techniques often require the time-consuming analysis, complicated operation procedures, large sample volumes, sophisticated instrumentation, and/or specialized skills. Therefore, the development of more simple, precise, and rapid analysis methods for the determination of Ag+ ions is still highly needed. As the typical detection alternatives, a variety of new sensing platforms have been developed for probing Ag+ ions including electrochemical method [14], colorimetric detection [15,16], fluorescent analysis method [17], and so on. Among these detection methods, the fluorescent analysis route has attracted great attentions because of its high sensitivity, high accuracy, and essential simplicity [18].

Moreover, with the rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, the fluorescent nanomaterials have emerged with some distinguished advantages in the design of various fluorescent sensors [19–21]. Among various nanomaterials, carbon-based nanomaterials have received much attention in recent years [22–24]. Carbon-based dots like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon nanodots (CDs) are a new form of zero-dimensional carbonaceous nanomaterials [25,26]. They have drawn increasing attentions owing to their exceptional advantages such as high optical absorptivity, chemical stability, biocompatibility, and low toxicity [27–31]. Particularly, some efforts have devoted to the analysis of Ag+ ions by using GQDs or CDs as the fluorescent probe based on either quenching (turn-off) [32,33] or enhancement (turn-on) [34,35] of the fluorescence intensities of GQDs/CDs. However, these single-intensity-based sensors can be usually challenged by some interfering factors, such as the probe concentration, instrument efficiency, and determination conditions. It is well known that the ratiometric fluorescence technique can avoid these interferents effectively, so that it can allow for the detection of analytes in the complex samples through recording the ratios of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths [36–38]. Particularly, the ratiometric fluorescence probes can also feature the advantages of improved analysis sensitivity and selectivity with the built-in correction for any background effects from the complex samples [39,40]. To the best our knowledge, however, the detection of Ag+ ions based on the ratiometric fluorescence responses of GQDs or CDs has hardly been reported up to date.

Recent studies have shown that Ag+ ions can oxidize the colorless o-phenlyenediamine (OPD) into the yellow 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) showing a strong fluorescence with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at 417 and 557nm, respectively [41,42]. Moreover, the π-network of thick planar sheets in sp2-bonded GQDs can provide a good substrate for anchoring π-conjugated molecules through π-π stacking interaction [43,44]. Importantly, there is a spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of GQDs and the absorption spectrum of DAP, so that the fluorescene quenching of GQDs by DAP could occur through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Inspired by these pioneering facts, herein, we seek to fabricate a sensitive and highly selective ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for probing Ag+ ions initially by using GQDs as the reference fluorophore and OPD as the specific recognition probe. As shown in Scheme 1 , when OPD was oxidized by Ag+ ions to form DAP, of which the fluorescence intensity at 557nm could greatly increase with the increasing concentrations of Ag+ ions, while the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was quenched simultaneously by the generating DAP through FRET. The developed ratiometric fluorescent Ag+ nanosensor has thereby been demonstrated with high detection selectivity and sensitivity, thus promising the practical applications for the analysis of real samples of lake water and tap waters.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 1.0mgmL−1) were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd. (http://www.xfnano.com/). The o-phenylenediamine (OPD), AgNO3, ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), Na2HPO4·12H2O, and NaH2PO4·2H2O were purchased from Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). 2,3-Diaminophenazine (DAP) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co. Ltd. 10mM phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) were prepared by mixing stock standard solutions of Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4. Lake water samples were obtained from Weishan Lake of Jining, Shandong province, China. All reagents were of analytical reagent grade, and used as received. Doubly deionized water was used throughout.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips CM200 FEG microscope. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were captured using a MultiMode Nanoscope IIIa controller atomic force microscope (Veeco, USA). All fluorescence measurements were carried out on an F-7000 spectrometer (Hi-tachi, Japan) operated at an excitation wavelength at 383nm, with both excitation and emission slit widths of 5.0nm. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a Varian cary-300 UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical run for the determination of Ag+ ions, 944μL of 10mM PBS (pH 6.0), 30μL of 6.0mM OPD, and 16μL of 6.0mM Ag+ ions were transferred into a 1.5mL eppendorf tube and then the mixture was thoroughly stirred at 25°C for 10min in dark. After that, 10μL of 1.0mgmL−1 GQDs solution was added into the mixture. After 5min incubation at 25°C in dark, the fluorescence spectra were measured for quantitative analysis of Ag+ ions.

For the fluorescence detections, the fluorescence spectra were recorded from 405nm to 650nm. The fluorescence intensities of GQDs at 445nm (I 445) and DAP at 557nm (I 557) were recorded. The ratiometric fluorescence signal variations at I 557/I 445 were utilized for sensitive and highly selective determination of Ag+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the present method for real water sample analysis was examined by lake water and tap water samples. Before detection, lake water samples were filtered through a 0.45μm filtered membrane and then centrifuged at 4000rpm for 15min. The tap water samples were detected directly without any pretreatment. Both the lake water and tape water samples were spiked by Ag+ ions at different concentration levels and then analyzed with the proposed analysis method.

To explore the properties of GQDs, UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and structural characterizations were carried out (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1A, GQDs exhibited the broad absorption band with a typical absorption peak at around 300nm, which was ascribed to the π-π* electronic transition in CC sp2 carbon domains of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials resulting in the strong fluorescence of GQDs [45]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra indicate that GQDs exhibited an obvious, narrow, and symmetrical fluorescence spectrum with a strong emission peak centered at 445nm under the excitation at 383nm. As observed from most of the carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, GQDs undoubtedly displayed the identical excitation-dependent fluorescence property, presumably resulting from the different surface state emission trap of these carbon-based fluorescent nanomterials [46,47]. As manifested in Fig. 1B, with the excitation wavelengths increasing from 330nm to 410nm, the maximum fluorescence peaks of GQDs shifted gradually to the longer wavelength direction obviously, where the fluorescence peak intensities of GQDs firstly increased and then decreased subsequently. These results indicate that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of GQDs could be observed at 383nm and 445nm, respectively.

Moreover, as can be seen from Fig. 2 A, the low-magnification TEM image reveals that these GQDs were well-dispersed and well separated from each other, which is further evidenced by the high-magnification image (Fig. S1). The corresponding particle size distribution histograms (Fig. 2B) indicated that these GQDs have diameters ranging from 1.0 to 5.0nm, and the average size is about 2.9nm in diameter. Furthermore, the atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was performed for the GQD assemblies (Fig. 2C), showing the heights were between 1.2 and 4.0nm that can correspond to 1-3 graphene layers, which is consistent to those previously reported [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensing performance for Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the performance of the ratiometric fluorescent analysis for Ag+ ions using GQDs was assessed, with the results shown in Fig. 3 . It was noted that GQDs themselves exhibited a strong fluorescence (curve 1). Moreover, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs could change slightly in the presence of Ag+ ions (curve 2) and OPD (curve 3) alone. However, the addition of Ag+ into GQDs-OPD system caused the distinctly different changes of fluorescence spectra (curve 4). The fluorescence intensities at 445nm could be quenched dramatically, while the fluorescence intensities at 557nm increased remarkably. The corresponding digital images (Inset of Fig. 3A) also revealed the obvious color changes after the addition of Ag+ into the GQDs-OPD system. These results indicated the possibility of the developed method for the detection of Ag+.

The underlying mechanism was further explored. According to the previous report, OPD could be oxidized to DAP by Ag+, and Ag+ were reduced to zero-valent silver and formed silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The so formed AgNPs could catalyze the reaction between OPD and Ag+ [41]. The reaction products of OPD and Ag+ were firstly characterized by TEM analysis. As shown in Fig. S2, some nanoparticles formed on a membrane layer. To confirm the components that might comprise such nanoparticles, the thus-formed nanoparticles were characterized by energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. S3, the element comprised in the nanoparticles was silver. These results indicated the formation of AgNPs could occur during the reaction of Ag+ and OPD. Then, the reaction products of Ag+ and OPD were characterized by the spectral analysis. As shown in Fig. S4, Ag+ ions or OPD can present no obvious UV–vis absorption in the range of 350–700nm. However, after Ag+ ions and OPD were incubated for 10min, the mixture could display an obvious UV–vis absorption in the range of 350–700nm with a maximum absorption peak at 417nm. In addition, the excitation/emission spectra of the products of Ag+ and OPD were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, both the maximum excitation and the maximum emission wavelengths were in according with those of DAP. These results indicated the generation of DAP which showed the maximum emission of fluorescence at 557nm [41,42]. Since there is a spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of GQDs and the absorption spectrum of DAP (Fig. 3B), a quenching mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is thus proposed for the above fluorescence issues (Scheme 1).

Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of DAP were recorded (Fig. S6). One can find that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was drastically quenched in the presence of DAP, whereas the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 557nm significantly increased despite GQDs presented, thus leading to a ratiometric fluorescent change between GQDs and DAP. The results were well consistent with the works reported previously [43]. Accordingly, the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 were varied tremendously with the increased Ag+ concentrations. That is, the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 could depend on the Ag+ concentrations. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescent analysis approach could be expected for the selective detection of Ag+ ions.

The detection conditions of the proposed ratiometric analysis strategy for Ag+ ions were optimized including the reaction time, pH values, and the OPD concentrations (Fig. 4 ). First, the effects of reaction time between Ag+ ions and OPD on the fluorescent Ag+ responses were studied with the experimental results shown in Fig. 4A. It was found that the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 increased gradually and then tended to be constant during 10min of reaction time. In other words, the oxidation reaction of Ag+ ions and OPD could be completed within 10min. Second, the effects of reaction time between the generated DAP and GQDs were also studied. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 could become constant during 10min of reaction time, indicating that the very fast reaction between DAP and GQDs could be expected. Third, the influences of pH values on the ratiometric Ag+ determination were evaluated using different pH values. As shown in Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensities of GQDs themselves changed slightly in the pH range of 4.0–9.0, indicating that GQDs were very stable in such a pH range. One can see from Fig. 4C that the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 firstly increased with the increasing pH values and then decreased gradually with the largest ratios at the pH 6.0. Of note, the ratios of fluorescence intensity might be extremely small under acidic or alkaline conditions (i.e., pH values below 6.0 or over 7.0). In strong acidic medium, the amino groups of OPD could be protonated, which could inhibit the oxidation by Ag+. When the pH value was higher than 6.0, Ag+ might be more prone to hydrolysis, which could decrease its oxidization ability. Moreover, the pH 6.0 was established to fit for enzyme mimetic reaction. Therefore, pH 6.0 was selected as the optimal condition for the sensing system. Lastly, the OPD concentrations could affect the total amounts of the produced DAP that could be directly related with the sensitivity of the detection of Ag+ ions. Therefore, the concentrations of OPD were evaluated with the data shown in Fig. 4D. It was discovered that the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 increased substantially with the increasing concentrations of OPD, and then decreased gradually. The maximum fluorescence intensity ratio of I 557/I 445 was obtained when the concentration of OPD was 180μM. Therefore, 180μM of OPD was chosen for the next experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection of Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimization conditions, we evaluated the capability of the developed ratiometric system for the quantitative detection of Ag+ ions (Fig. 5 ). Fig. 5A displayed the typical fluorescence spectra of GQDs-OPD system as the functions of increasing concentrations of Ag+ ions. It was observed that the fluorescence intensities of GQDs at 445nm were quenched continuously with the gradual increase of the fluorescence intensities of DAP at 557nm upon the addition of Ag+ ions with the increasing concentrations, obviously indicating that ratiometric fluorescence variation was mainly dependent on the concentrations of Ag+ ions. Moreover, the plotting of the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 against the Ag+ concentrations was shown in Fig. 5B. The fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 could exhibit a good linear responses to Ag+ ions in the concentration range of 0–115.2μM, which was described by the regression equation of I 557/I 445 =0.08887+0.01649C (R2 =0.9945). Moreover, the color changes of GQDs-OPD system in the absence and presence of various amounts of Ag+ were recorded by using a digital camera (Inset of Fig. 5B). The color change of the GQDs-OPD system induced by Ag+ can be visualized by the naked eye.

The relative standard deviation for five replicate measurements of 96.0μM Ag+ was calculated to be 3.21%, with the detection limit of 250nM (S/N=3). In addition, we compared the experimental results with those of other fluorimetric methods for Ag+ detection using different fluorescent probes. As shown in Table 1 , the developed fluorimetric method showed better linear ranges than others. Moreover, it could provide better analysis sensitivity in comparison with those alternatively using carbon quantum dots [33] and DNA-derived bio-dots [51].

The analysis selectivity of the developed ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for Ag+ ions was valuated with the results shown in Fig. 6 . After the addition of OPD and GQDs, the fluorescence intensity ratios of I 557/I 445 changed slightly for the samples containing Hg2+ ions and obviously for the samples containing Ag+ and Cu2+ ions, while the samples containing other 10 metal ions showed no obvious change. Then, the influences of metal ions on the fluorescence intensities of GQDs were investigated, with the data shown in Fig. S8. Obviously, most of metal ions had negligible influences on the fluorescence intensities of GQDs. Herein, Hg2+ could quench the fluorescence intensity of GQDs seriously, while Cu2+ could quench nearly 50% fluorescence intensity of GQDs. However, Hg2+ could not oxidize OPD into DAP (Fig. S9). Thus, the developed fluorimetric method can distinguish Ag+ from Hg2+ ions. It was further evidenced by the corresponding digital photos (Inset of Fig. 6). As previously reported, Cu2+ also could oxidize OPD into DAP (Fig. S9) [41]. Thus, Cu2+ ions may have a risk of interfereing the detection of Ag+ ions. However, the addition of Cu2+ ions into the GQDs-OPD system in the presence of EDTA gave no change in the fluorescence intensity ratio (Fig. S10). Also note that no obvious fluorescence intensity ratio of I 557/I 445 was observed after the addition of EDTA into the GQDs-OPD-Ag+ solution, thus indicating the weak effect of EDTA on the detection of Ag+ ions in the developed ratiometric detection system. These results indicate that the developed GQDs-OPD system can be used for the detection of Ag+ ions with high analysis selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical analysis of environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application feasibility of the developed ratiometric GQDs-OPD system was evaluated for Ag+ ions in real samples of tap water and lake water, with the data summarized in Table 2 . Here, the water samples were spiked with Ag+ ions of different concentrations to be analyzed with the proposed ratiometric method. It was found that the analysis recoveries were desirably satisfactory, demonstrating that this developed sensor can possess the great potential in the practical applications for probing Ag+ ions in environmental water samples.

In summary, we have developed a highly selective and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for probing Ag+ ions in real water samples. The developed Ag+ nanosensor can compose of two sections: GQDs as the reference fluorophore and OPD as the recognition probe serving as the response signal source. The analysis protocol can show the high analysis selectivity for Ag+ ions with the considerably low interferences of other metal ions from the sample background. The practical application feasibility of the developed ratiometric fluorescent analysis strategy was demonstrated for the determination of Ag+ ions in real water samples. This simple, rapid, and effective analysis strategy may extend the applications of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials in the biological and environmental detection or monitoring fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is kindly supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405094, 81303179, and 21675099), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2013BQ018), the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (No. SKLEAC201506), the National Undergraduate Research Training Program (No. 201610446033), and the Taishan Scholar Foundation of Shandong Province, China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.06.086 .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanostructured sensors for detection of heavy metals: a review
High-sensitivity detection of silver ions using oligonucleotide-immobilized oscillator
Bioaccumulation and toxicity of silver compounds: a review
Development of a highly sensitive and selective optical sensor for determination of ultra-trace amount of silver ions
Facile and rapid synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent gold nanoclusters for sensitive and selective detection of Ag+
Preconcentration of traces of gold, silver and palladium on activated carbon and its determination in geological samples by liame AAS after wet ashing
Application of 2-mercaptobenzothiazole-modified silica gel to on-line preconcentration and separation of silver for its atomic absorption spectrometric determination
Synthesis of achitosan- based chelating resin and its application to the selective concentration and ultratrace determination of silver in environmental waters samples
Panoramic analysis for monitoring trace metals in natural waters by ICP-MS
Analytical procedures for the determination of selected trace elements in peat and plant samples by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry
Fiber-optic microsensor array based on fluorescent bulk optode microspheres for the trace analysis of silver ions
5,10,15-Tris(pentafluorophenyl)corrole as highly selective neutral carrier for a silver ion-sensitive electrode
A singal-on electrochemical biosensor for sensitive detection of silver ion based on alkanethiol-cabron nanotube-oligonucleotide modified electrodes
A colorimetric and fluorescence turn-off chemosensor for the detection of silver ion based on a conjugated polymer containing 2,3-di(pyridine-2-yl)quinoxaline
New coumarin-based dual chromogenic probe: Naked eye detection of copper and silver ions
Two novel dyes containing spirobifluorene and triphenylamine: Synthesis, one- and two-photon excited fluorescence and applications as probes for silver ions, water and cell imaging
Sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions by fluorescent Ag nanoclusters synthesized via a hydrothermal method
Rapid selective, and ultrasensitive fluorimetric analysis of mercury and copper levels in blood using bimetallic gold-silver nanoclusters with silver effect-enhanced red fluorescence

Lab-on-a-drop: biocompatible fluorescent nanoprobes of gold nanoclusters for label-free evaluation of phosphorylation-induced inhibition of acetylcholinesterase activity towards the ultrasensitive detection of pesticide residues
Polymerizing dopamine onto Q-graphene scaffolds towards the fluorescent nanocomposites with high aqueous stability and enhanced fluorescence for the fluorescence analysis and imaging of copper ions
Colorimetric platform for visual detection of cancer biomarker based on intrinsic peroxidase activity of graphene oxide
Electrochemical immunosensor based on electron transfer mediated by graphene oxide initiated silver enhancement

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
A fluorometric assay for tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening based on graphene quantum dots
Fluorimetric evaluation of glutathione reductase activity and its inhibitors using carbon quantum dots
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Graphene oxide quantum dots@silver core-shell nanocrystals as turn-on fluorescent nanoprobe for ultrasensitive detection of prostate specific antigen
Niche nanoparticle-based FRET assay for bleomycin detection via DNA scission
Novel turn-on fluorescent detection of alkaline phosphatase based on green synthesized carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets
A turn-on fluorescent sensor for the detection of melamine based on the anti-quenching ability of Hg2+ to carbon nanodots
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Ag+ ions
Fluorescent carbon quantum dot hydrogels for direct determination of silver ions
One-pot sysnthesis of carbon nanodots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Ag+ based on the Ag+-induced enhancement of fluorescence
Highly luminescent N-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective multifunctional fluorescence sensing platform
Energy transfer cassettes based on organic fluorophores: construction and applications in ratiometric sensing
Functionalized silica nanoparticles: a platform for fluorescence imaging at the cell and small animal levels
Design, synthesis and biological application of chemical probes for bioimaging
Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+
Through bond energy transfer: a convenient and universal strategy toward efficient ratiometric fluorescent probe for bioimaging applications
A nanoparticle autocatalytic sensor for Ag+ and Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity and its application in test paper
Label-free fluorescence detection of mercury ions based on the regulation of the Ag autocatalytic reaction
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase
Label-free and ratiometric detection of nuclei acids based on graphene quantum dots utilizing cascade amplification by nicking endonuclease and catalytic G-quadruplex DNAzyme
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots: origin of tunable heterogeneous photoluminescence
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Multi-positively charged dendrimeric nanoparticles induced fluorescence quenching of graphene quantum dots for heparin and chondroitin sulfate detection
Label-free fluorescent DNA sensor for the detection of silver ions based on molecular light switch Ru complex and unmodified quantum dots
DNA derived fluorescent bio-dots for sensitive detection of mercury and silver ions in aqueous solution
Microwave-assisted synthesis of BSA-protected small gold nanoclusters and their fluorescence-enhanced sensing of silver(I) ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xian-En Zhao received his PhD from Northwest Institute of Plateau Biology of the Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2009, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as an associate professor in Chemistry. His current research interests include biosensors, mass spectrometry analytical methodology, and pharmaceutical analysis.

Cuihua Lei received her bachelor degree in Chemistry Application from Wei Fang University in 2015, and now is a postgraduate student in Analytical Chemistry at Qufu Normal University. Her research interests focus on the synthesis and analytical application of carbon nanomaterials.
Yue Gao is presently studying for her bachelor degree in Chemistry Education at Qufu Normal University.
Han Gao is presently studying for her bachelor degree in Chemistry Education at Qufu Normal University.

Shuyun Zhu received her PhD from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry in 2012, and now is working at Qufu Normal University as an associate professor in Chemistry. Her current research interests include nanomaterials, as well as biosensors based on DNA and enzymes.
Xue Yang is presently studying for her bachelor degree in Chemistry Education at QuFu Normal University.

Jinmao You is a professor of analytical chemistry at Qufu Normal University. He has published over 300 papers and 50 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, HPLC, fluorescence, and analysis of natural products.

Hua Wang received his PhD from Hunan University in 2004 and now is working at Qufu Normal University as a professor in Chemistry. His research interests mainly include chemo/biosensors, advanced functional materials, and organic synthesis.
